Plain Language Summary of Publication
First draft submitted: 23 May 2024; Accepted for publication: 23 May 2025
Plain language summary of the THOR Cohort 1 
study comparing treatment with erdafitinib or 
chemotherapy in advanced or metastatic 
­urothelial cancer
What is this summary about? 
This is a plain language summary of an article published in The New England 
Journal of Medicine in 2023 describing results from Cohort 1 of the THOR study.
The study looked at whether a medication called erdafitinib was safe and 
effective at increasing the length of time that participants with a type of bladder 
and upper/lower tract cancer called urothelial cancer were alive compared to 
chemotherapy treatment.
•	 Participants had advanced or metastatic unresectable urothelial cancer with c­ertain characteristics.
•	 Participants’ cancer must have certain genetic alterations in 1 or both of 2 related genes named fibroblast growth factor receptor 2 
(FGFR2) and 3 (FGFR3).
•	 The cancer must have grown or spread after participants received 1 or 2 previous cancer treatments that included an i­mmunotherapy 
medication.
Additional effective treatment options are needed for people after immunotherapy.
What were the results of the study?
•	 The median overall survival was 12.1 months for erdafitinib versus 7.8 months for chemotherapy. In the erdafitinib group, an 
e­stimated 85% of participants were still alive at 6 months and an estimated 51% were still alive after 12 months.
•	 The median progression-free survival was 5.6 months for participants treated with erdafitinib versus 2.7 months for those treated 
with chemotherapy.
•	 The objective response rate was 45.6% in the erdafitinib group and 11.5% in the 
c­hemotherapy group.
Summary
Yohann Loriota, Spyros Triantosb, Kris Deprincec & Arlene O. Siefker-Radtked
aDepartment of Cancer Medicine, INSERM U981, Gustave Roussy, Université Paris-Saclay, Villejuif, France; bJanssen Research & Development, Spring House, PA, USA;
cJanssen Research & Development, Beerse, Belgium; dDepartment of Genitourinary Medical Oncology, The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, 
TX, USA
You can read the original article 'Erdafitinib or chemotherapy in advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma', published in The New 
England Journal of Medicine, for free at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2308849

1985
Plain Language Summary of Publication  Loriot, Triantos, Deprince and Siefker-Radtke
Urothelial cancer is a type of cancer that starts in the lining of the urinary tract and can affect the bladder and other sections of the 
urinary tract. The urinary tract is made up of the kidneys, the bladder, and tubes that link the kidneys to the bladder (ureters) and 
the bladder to the outside of the body allowing urine to exit (urethra) (for more information on urothelial cancer and the journey 
of people with urothelial cancer, please refer to 'Plain language summary of erdafitinib in locally advanced or metastatic urothelial 
­carcinoma: a phase 2 study with long-term follow-up' at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.2217/fon-2023-0596).
Urothelial cancer is referred to as metastatic or unresectable when it has spread to various other parts of the body, including bones, 
lungs, and the liver, and can no longer be removed by surgery.
What is urothelial cancer?
Erdafitinib is used to treat the condition under study that is discussed in this summary. Approval varies by country; please check with 
your doctor for more details.
People with metastatic or unresectable 
urothelial cancer with certain FGFR3 
gene alterations, their families, patient 
advocates, caregivers and loved ones, 
and healthcare professionals.
The THOR study was sponsored by Janssen Research & Development, LLC, a 
Johnson & Johnson company. Erdafitinib is manufactured for Janssen Products, 
LP, Horsham, PA, USA, by Janssen-Cilag SpA, Latina, Italy.
What is the purpose of this plain language summary?
Who should read this article?
Who sponsored this study?
Sponsor: A company or organization that oversees and pays for a clinical 
research study. The sponsor also collects and analyzes the information from 
the study.
•	 Common side effects of erdafitinib were manageable and included high phosphate levels in the blood (hyperphosphatemia), 
d­iarrhea, an inflamed and sore mouth, and nail disorders.
What do the results of the study mean?
•	 The THOR Cohort 1 study found that treatment with erdafitinib extended the amount of time that participants were alive compared 
with chemotherapy treatment.
•	 After a pre-planned analysis determined that participants had better outcomes with erdafitinib, those receiving chemotherapy 
were allowed to switch to erdafitinib.
•	 Based on these results, erdafitinib was approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for adults with advanced or 
m­etastatic urothelial cancer with certain FGFR3 gene alterations whose cancer continued to grow after at least 1 previous ­systemic 
treatment, including chemotherapy/immunotherapy.
•	 Erdafitinib is also approved in other countries to treat advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer; please check with your doctor for 
more details.
Future Oncol. (2025) 21(16)
1986
Erdafitinib or chemotherapy in advanced or metastatic ­urothelial cancer 
Plain Language Summary of Publication
www.tandfonline.com
There are 3 main types of treatment available:
•	 Chemotherapy
•	 Immunotherapy
•	 Targeted therapy, including antibody-drug conjugates.
Platinum-based chemotherapy is usually the first treatment given to people (first-line therapy) with newly diagnosed advanced and 
metastatic urothelial cancer. Cisplatin-based chemotherapy is a type of chemotherapy that contains a drug called cisplatin. Many 
people cannot receive this type of therapy due to other medical conditions. Carboplatin-based chemotherapy, another type of 
platinum-based chemotherapy, can sometimes be used to treat these people.
Immunotherapy is also sometimes used as first-line therapy for people who are not able to receive cisplatin, as maintenance therapy 
after platinum-based chemotherapy to help delay or prevent cancer from returning after it stopped growing, or as second-line therapy 
for people when first-line therapy did not work or stopped working and their cancer continued to grow following chemotherapy.
Enfortumab vedotin, an antibody-drug conjugate, was recently approved in combination with pembrolizumab as first-line therapy 
for metastatic urothelial cancer, regardless of cisplatin eligibility. Enfortumab vedotin can also be given after platinum-based 
chemotherapy and after immunotherapy. Other antibody-drug conjugates that can be used include sacituzumab govitecan.
Second-line treatment options also include the targeted therapy erdafitinib, an FGFR inhibitor, and other types of chemotherapy. 
FGFR inhibitors are medications that work by blocking the activity of FGFR (discussed in the "What is erdafitinib?" section of the 'Plain 
language summary of erdafitinib in locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma: a phase 2 study with long-term follow-up').
Further therapy includes participation in clinical trials or best supportive care, which involves treatment that is focused on managing 
symptoms and keeping people comfortable, even if their cancer cannot be further treated.
How is advanced and metastatic urothelial cancer treated?

1987
Plain Language Summary of Publication  Loriot, Triantos, Deprince and Siefker-Radtke
What are FGFR gene alterations?
In healthy cells, the family of proteins called FGFRs is involved in important normal cell processes.
However, FGFR can undergo changes in its DNA called FGFR gene alterations. FGFR gene alterations can be mutations, which are any 
change in the DNA sequence, or fusions, in which a second gene is combined (merged) with the FGFR gene. FGFR gene alterations 
may lead to cancer.
FGFR gene alterations are found in around 20% of advanced or metastatic urothelial cancers (for more information on FGFR gene 
alterations, please refer to 'Plain language summary of erdafitinib in locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma: a phase 2 
study with long-term follow-up' at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.2217/fon-2023-0596).
In normal healthy bladder cells, FGFR controls cell growth. However, cells with FGFR alterations may lose control of their growth and 
this may lead to bladder cancer.
Erdafitinib is a type of targeted therapy that blocks the growth and survival of advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer cells with 
certain FGFR gene alterations. In clinical trials, erdafitinib has been successful in shrinking the size of tumors in participants with 
advanced or metastatic unresectable urothelial cancer.
Erdafitinib is the only FGFR inhibitor approved by the FDA in the United States to treat locally advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer 
in adults with certain FGFR gene alterations whose cancer has kept growing despite having received at least 1 prior systemic treatment 
(treatment that acts throughout the entire body). Erdafitinib is not recommended for the treatment of people eligible for and have 
not received prior immunotherapy (for more information on erdafitinib, please refer to 'Plain language summary of erdafitinib in 
locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma: a phase 2 study with long-term follow-up' at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/
abs/10.2217/fon-2023-0596).
Erdafitinib is also approved in other countries to treat advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer.
What is erdafitinib and how does it work?
Researchers wanted to know if erdafitinib could increase the length of time participants were alive after the start of treatment, p­revent 
or delay the growth and spread of cancer, or decrease tumor size compared with chemotherapy. In addition, researchers wanted to 
identify the side effects of treatment.
What was the THOR Cohort 1 study designed to look at?

How was the THOR Cohort 1 study carried out?
•	 The first participant in Cohort 1 was assigned to treatment on August 6, 2018, and data for this review of results were collected on 
January 15, 2023.
•	 During the study, the levels of phosphate in participants’ blood were monitored. An increase in phosphate levels in the blood is an 
indication that erdafitinib is working.

•	 The characteristics of participants, such as age and sex, were balanced between the treatment groups.
•	 All the participants had previous treatment with immunotherapy, with the exception of 3 participants who had been 
­included ­incorrectly.
To allow detection and measuring of tumors inside the body, special imaging techniques like computerized tomography (CT) scans 
and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans were used to take pictures of the chest, abdomen, pelvis, and other parts of the body 
where disease was present. These pictures were taken at specific time points: every 6 weeks for the first 6 months of treatment, then 
every 12 weeks for the next 6 months and beyond. After the first year, scans were performed as needed.
Vital sign measurements, physical and eye examinations, and laboratory tests such as electrocardiograms (test to check the heart) 
and blood tests were used to monitor for side effects.
After treatment was finished, the participants were contacted every 12 weeks to check on their overall condition.
How did researchers measure the effectiveness of erdafitinib?

•	 After the review of data collected up to a specified point in the study (interim analysis), medical experts in urothelial cancer who were 
responsible for monitoring the study determined that participants had better outcomes with erdafitinib than with c­hemotherapy 
and participants receiving chemotherapy were allowed to switch to erdafitinib, and the study was stopped.
Future Oncol. (2025) 21(16)
1992
Erdafitinib or chemotherapy in advanced or metastatic ­urothelial cancer 
Plain Language Summary of Publication
www.tandfonline.com
•	 135 patients in the erdafitinib group and 112 patients in the chemotherapy group received at least 1 dose of study treatment.
•	 Almost all participants in the study had at least 1 side effect.
•	 Side effects were rated using a scale from 1 to 5. Grade 1 and 2 side effects were considered mild or moderate. Grade 3–4 side effects 
severely limited a person’s ability to care for themselves and usually needed medical intervention and sometimes hospitalization. 
Grade 5 side effects were fatal.
•	 The most common side effects in the erdafitinib group were high phosphate levels in the blood (hyperphosphatemia) (80%), ­diarrhea 
(62%), an inflamed and sore mouth (48%), and nail disorders (67%). These side effects were treated in various ways (d­iscussed in the 
“How were erdafitinib side effects managed in the study?” section).
•	 Side effects observed with erdafitinib were similar to those in the BLC2001 study of erdafitinib (for more information on e­rdafitinib 
side effects and their management, please refer to 'Plain language summary of erdafitinib in locally advanced or metastatic 
­urothelial carcinoma: a phase 2 study with long-term follow-up' at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.2217/fon-2023-0596).
•	 Common side effects in the chemotherapy group were shortage of red blood cells (anemia) (32%), constipation (28%), and ­weakness 
or lack of energy (25%).
•	 The frequency of severe side effects was similar with erdafitinib (63%) and chemotherapy (64%).
•	 Side effects of interest are those known to be associated with erdafitinib and other medications in this class of FGFR inhibitors. Side 
effects of interest included nail and skin disorders, central serous retinopathy (CSR), and other eye disorders.
•	 Side effects that were related to treatment and led to death were more common with chemotherapy than with erdafitinib.
What were the most common side effects in the THOR Cohort 1 study?

Plain Language Summary of Publication  Loriot, Triantos, Deprince and Siefker-Radtke
What measures were taken to treat these common side effects of erdafitinib in the study?
High phosphate levels in the blood (hyperphosphatemia)
•	 Phosphate levels in the blood were monitored and participants were advised to decrease phosphate intake by limiting food high in 
phosphate and certain medications.
•	 Erdafitinib dose changes or discontinuation of treatment was recommended if blood phosphate levels were consistently high.
•	 A medicine called phosphate binders that traps blood phosphate was given, if needed, to reduce blood phosphate levels.
Nail disorders
•	 Nail disorders were managed with preventive measures, which included keeping nails trimmed and fingers and toes clean and 
wearing comfortable shoes and gloves for chores.
•	 Application of a medicine called silver nitrate, vinegar soaks, and antibiotic or antifungal medicines were used to treat nail i­nfections.
Skin disorders
•	 Skin disorders were managed by limiting exposure to sunlight, using sunscreen, moisturizers, emollient creams, and cotton clothes, 
and limiting bathing and use of perfumed products.
•	 Medicated creams were used to treat symptoms.
Eye disorders and CSR
•	 Regular vision tests were recommended to manage CSR and other eye disorders.
•	 Erdafitinib treatment was paused until eye examinations were done and findings on eye exams were resolved. Depending on 
the seriousness of these findings and whether they improved or resolved after pausing erdafitinib, erdafitinib dose changes or 
­permanent discontinuation of treatment was recommended.
Which side effects led to participants stopping study treatment?

The THOR Cohort 1 study showed that erdafitinib was more effective in keeping participants with advanced or metastatic urothelial 
cancer with certain FGFR3 gene alterations after immunotherapy alive longer than standard chemotherapy.
•	 Erdafitinib treatment increased median overall survival and median progression-free survival of participants compared with 
c­hemotherapy treatment.
•	 Side effects observed with erdafitinib were similar to those that occurred in the previous BLC2001 study of erdafitinib.
•	 The THOR Cohort 1 study supports the clinical effectiveness of erdafitinib as the standard of care option for people with advanced 
or metastatic urothelial cancer with certain FGFR3 gene alterations after previous immunotherapy.
•	 Updated bladder cancer guidelines now recommend erdafitinib as a second-line treatment option for people with advanced 
or metastatic urothelial cancer with certain FGFR3 gene alterations after previous systemic therapy, including chemotherapy/
immunotherapy.
What do the results of this study mean?

JMdo007290/full/?relatedArticle=10.1056/NEJMoa2308849
The THOR Cohort 1 study started on August 6, 2018, and data for this review of results were collected on January 15, 2023.
You can read more about the THOR study at the following trial registration sites:
•	  https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT03390504 
•	  https://www.clinicaltrialsregister.eu/ctr-search/search?query=2017-002932-18
You can read more about erdafitinib in the previously published article titled 'Plain language summary of erdafitinib in locally 
­advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma: a phase 2 study with long-term follow-up', available to read at: https://www.tandfonline.
com/doi/abs/10.2217/fon-2023-0596
Erdafitinib is being studied further as a component of a new intravesical erdafitinib releasing system, TAR-210, that can be placed 
directly within the bladder.
